markdown.markdown:
## Overview The NGS Figure Generator is an interactive tool for transforming high-throughput sequencing outputs into publication-ready figures. It enables users to generate, customize, and refine visualizations to clearly communicate key biological insights. --- ## Problem High-throughput sequencing workflows often produce complex outputs that require extensive post-processing to visualize effectively. Researchers frequently: - spend significant time manually formatting figures for publication - rely on static plots that are difficult to iteratively refine - lack intuitive tools for exploring and highlighting key signals This creates a gap between **data generation** and **effective scientific communication**. --- ## Approach I developed this tool to streamline the transition from processed sequencing data to publication-quality figures. The design emphasizes: - rapid generation of standard bioinformatics plots - interactive adjustment of visualization parameters - reproducible workflows for consistent figure generation --- ## Key Features - **Interactive figure generation** - dynamically adjust thresholds and visualization parameters - **Multiple visualization types** - support for volcano plots and heatmaps for expression analysis - **Customizable outputs** - refine annotations, clustering, and visual emphasis - **Publication-ready formatting** - generate clean, structured figures suitable for manuscripts - **Extensible architecture** - adaptable to multiple sequencing-based analyses --- ## Example Use Cases - Differential expression visualization (e.g., volcano plots) - Expression pattern analysis across samples (heatmaps) - Exploratory analysis of gene-level signals - Comparative visualization across experimental conditions --- ## Interactive Visualization --- ### Differential Expression Visualization - Interactive Volcano Plot This example demonstrates a volcano plot generated from differential expression analysis. Users can explore differential expression results and dynamically identify significant genes. ```{python} #| echo: false #| fig-width: 8 #| fig-height: 5 import plotly.graph_objects as go import numpy as np import pandas as pd np.random.seed(42) # Generate data genes = [f"Gene{i}" for i in range(1, 101)] log2fc = np.concatenate([ np.random.normal(0, 0.5, size=70), np.random.uniform(-3, 3, size=30) ]) pvalues = np.concatenate([ np.random.uniform(0.05, 0.5, size=70), np.random.uniform(0.0001, 0.05, size=30) ]) neg_log10_pval = -np.log10(pvalues) df = pd.DataFrame({ "gene": genes, "log2fc": log2fc, "pvalue": pvalues, "neg_log10_pval": neg_log10_pval }) # Threshold p_thresh = 0.05 fc_thresh = 1 up = (df["log2fc"] > fc_thresh) & (df["pvalue"] < p_thresh) down = (df["log2fc"] < -fc_thresh) & (df["pvalue"] < p_thresh) background = ~(up | down) fig = go.Figure() # Upregulated (red) fig.add_trace(go.Scatter( x=df.loc[up, "log2fc"], y=df.loc[up, "neg_log10_pval"], mode='markers', marker=dict(color='red', size=8), name='Upregulated', text=df.loc[up, "gene"], hovertemplate="Gene: %{text}
log2FC: %{x:.2f}
-log10(p): %{y:.2f}" )) # Downregulated (blue) fig.add_trace(go.Scatter( x=df.loc[down, "log2fc"], y=df.loc[down, "neg_log10_pval"], mode='markers', marker=dict(color='blue', size=8), name='Downregulated', text=df.loc[down, "gene"], hovertemplate="Gene: %{text}
log2FC: %{x:.2f}
-log10(p): %{y:.2f}" )) # Background (gray) fig.add_trace(go.Scatter( x=df.loc[background, "log2fc"], y=df.loc[background, "neg_log10_pval"], mode='markers', marker=dict(color='lightgray', size=5), name='Not significant', text=df.loc[background, "gene"], hovertemplate="Gene: %{text}
log2FC: %{x:.2f}
-log10(p): %{y:.2f}" )) # Add threshold lines fig.add_hline(y=-np.log10(p_thresh),line_dash="solid", line_color="blue") fig.add_vline(x=1, line_dash="dash", line_color="black") fig.add_vline(x=-1, line_dash="dash", line_color="black") fig.update_layout( xaxis_title="Log2 Fold Change", yaxis_title="-Log10(p-value)", height=500, legend_title="Gene Classification" ) # Label top up- and down-regulated genes top_n = 5 top_up = df.loc[up].nlargest(top_n, "neg_log10_pval") top_down = df.loc[down].nlargest(top_n, "neg_log10_pval") top_genes = pd.concat([top_up, top_down]) # Sort so labels are spaced in order (prevents stacking) top_genes = top_genes.sort_values("neg_log10_pval", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True) for i, row in top_genes.iterrows(): # alternate vertical spacing vertical_offset = -20 - (i % 3) * 15 # push left/right depending on direction horizontal_offset = 30 if row["log2fc"] > 0 else -30 fig.add_annotation( x=row["log2fc"], y=row["neg_log10_pval"], text=row["gene"], showarrow=True, arrowhead=2, ax=horizontal_offset, ay=vertical_offset, font=dict(size=10), bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.8)", bordercolor="black", borderwidth=1 ) fig ``` > Users can hover over points to view gene-level details and explore patterns in real time. --- ### Interactive Expression Heatmap This heatmap visualizes gene expression patterns across samples, enabling identification of clustering patterns and condition-specific signals. Users can refine clustering parameters and visualization settings to highlight key biological trends. --- ```{python} #| echo: false #| fig-width: 8 #| fig-height: 5 import plotly.graph_objects as go import pandas as pd import numpy as np # --- simulate the CSV users would upload --- df = pd.DataFrame({ "SampleID": ["Sample1", "Sample2", "Sample3", "Sample4", "Sample5"], "GeneA": [5.1, 7.4, 3.2, 6.5, 4.1], "GeneB": [2.3, 6.1, 2.1, 7.2, 3.5], "GeneC": [3.5, 5.9, 4.7, 5.8, 4.8], "GeneD": [4.8, 7.3, 3.9, 6.9, 5.2], "GeneE": [3.2, 6.8, 4.5, 6.4, 4.7] }) # Set SampleID as row labels and drop it from matrix df_matrix = df.set_index("SampleID") # Create heatmap fig = go.Figure( data=go.Heatmap( z=df_matrix.values, x=df_matrix.columns, # Genes y=df_matrix.index, # Samples colorscale="Viridis", colorbar=dict(title="Expression"), hovertemplate= "Sample: %{y}
" + "Gene: %{x}
" + "Expression: %{z:.2f}" ) ) fig.update_layout( height=500 ) fig ``` --- ### Customizable Figure Refinement The tool enables iterative refinement of figures, allowing users to modify parameters such as thresholds, clustering behavior, labeling, and visual emphasis to produce publication-ready outputs. --- ## Technical Implementation The NGS Figure Generator is built as a modular web-based application that integrates data processing, visualization, and user interaction. ### Frontend - **Framework:** React (with component libraries such as shadcn/ui) - Provides a user-friendly interface for: - uploading sequencing data (CSV/TXT) - selecting visualization parameters - customizing figure outputs in real time ### Backend - **Language:** Python - **Frameworks:** Flask or FastAPI - Handles: - data ingestion and preprocessing - computation of analytical outputs - generation of visualizations ### Data Processing & Visualization - **Data handling:** pandas - **Statistical and ML methods:** scikit-learn (e.g., PCA) - **Visualization libraries:** - Matplotlib / Seaborn → static, publication-ready figures - Plotly → interactive visualizations ### Supported Visualizations - **Volcano & MA plots:** Matplotlib - **Heatmaps:** Seaborn / Matplotlib - **PCA:** scikit-learn (computation) + Plotly (interactive rendering) ### File Handling - Supports structured input formats (CSV, TXT) - Designed for integration with standard bioinformatics outputs ### Deployment & Reproducibility - Containerized using Docker for consistent environments - Deployable as a local server-based application --- ## Future Development Planned enhancements include: - support for user-uploaded datasets - additional plot types (PCA, heatmaps, enrichment plots) - enhanced export options for publication workflows --- ## GitHub Repository https://github.com/fraserclaire/seq-visualizer
